---
title: "{{< meta course_title >}}"
deck_id: d05
deck_title: "{{< meta decks.d05.title >}}"
deck_short_title: "{{< meta decks.d05.short_title >}}"

include-after-body:
  - text: |
      <script id="deck-nav-meta" type="application/json">
      {
        "deck_prev_file": "{{< meta decks.d04.file >}}",
        "deck_prev_title": "{{< meta decks.d04.title >}}",
        "deck_next_file": "",
        "deck_next_title": ""
      }
      </script>

subtitle: |
  <span class="deck-title">{{< meta decks.d05.title >}}</span><br>
  <span class="deck-meta">{{< meta texts.wms.short >}} Ch. 15<br>
  Project Presentations on April 20–22<br>
  Monday, May 4, 8:00 AM, in PH 108 <br>
  <span class="indent">Comprehensive Final Exam </span>
  </span>
---

# Why nonparametric methods?

- Our inferences thus far relied assumptions about the distribution of the data in terms of parameters (e.g., mean, variance):
  - Confidence intervals for means and variances assuming Bernoulli, exponential, normal, etc.
  - Hypothesis tests for means and variances assuming Bernoulli, exponential, normal, etc.
  - If the distribution is unknown, we have relied on the CLT and large $n$ to justify normal approximations
  - Regression models assuming that the response follows a prescribed distribution (e.g., normal, binomial) with parameters to be inferred

<h3>What if</h3>
  - Data are skewed?
  - Outliers are present?
  - Only ordering is trustworthy?

## Key idea

Replace raw data with

- signs

- ranks

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|------------|----------------|
| Fewer assumptions <br> Robustness | Loss of efficiency if parametric assumptions hold |


### Roadmap

| Setting | Parametric | Nonparametric |
|---|---|---|
| Matched pairs | Paired t-test | Sign, Wilcoxon signed-rank |
| Two samples | Two-sample t-test | Mann–Whitney |
| $m$ groups | One-way ANOVA | Kruskal–Wallis |

# Matched-pairs data

- Observations come in pairs: $(X_1,Y_1), \dots, (X_n,Y_n)$

- Define differences: $D_i = X_i - Y_i$

- Parametric approach: paired $t$-test uses $\barD$ and $S_D$ to make inferences about $\mu_D$ assuming $D_i$ are IID normal

- Nonparametric approach uses signs or ranks of $D_i$

## Sign test

### $\displaystyle H_0: P(D_i > 0) = \frac{1}{2}$, $\displaystyle H_A: P(D_i > 0) \neq \frac{1}{2}$

- Let $S = \#\{D_i > 0\}$

- Under $H_0: S \sim \Bin(n, 1/2)$ 

1. Compute $s = \#\{d_i > 0\}$ from data

1. Compute $p$-value $p = 2 \times \min\bigl(P(S \le s), P(S \ge s)\bigr)$

1. Reject $H_0$ if $p < \alpha$ 


### Example

Differences:
$$
D = [2, -3, 0, 5, -1, 4]
$$

- Remove zero → $n' = 5$
- Signs: $+,-,+,-,+$
- $S = 3$

Under $H_0$: $S \sim \Bin(5, 1/2)$

Compute p-value

## Wilcoxon signed-rank test

  1. Compute $D_i$
  2. Remove zeros
  3. Rank $|D_i|$
  4. Attach sign back

---

# Two independent samples

- Data:
  $$
  X_1,\dots,X_{n_1}, \quad Y_1,\dots,Y_{n_2}
  $$

- Nonparametric:
  - compare relative positions

---

# Mann–Whitney test

- Pool all observations
- Rank them
- Compare rank sums

---

# More than two groups

- Data:
  $$
  m \text{ groups}
  $$

- Nonparametric:
  - Kruskal–Wallis

---

# Summary

- Use signs and ranks
- Fewer assumptions
- More robust
